result:
On the third floor of the museum they’ve built for what slips away, there is a room with no doors and a single window. The docents will tell you this is an optical trick—the room has a door; your mind simply refuses to notice it, having been discouraged by years of glancing at exits and deciding they belong to other people in other emergencies. But it still feels like there is just the wide window cut into the wall, low enough that a child could rest their chin on the sill. Beyond it: a field in winter, grasses kneeling under a lid of snow, a row of trees whose thin black arms scrape faint lines into a sky so pale it could be a lost word for light.

Two laminated cards hang from threads on the wall. The first offers an explanation: The Room of Almost. The second gives a set of instructions. Look, but do not try to remember what you have lost, it says. It will come to you if it wants.

The museum is not a museum in the way you might think. It has no glass cases holding saints’ bones or beads strung from the hair of conquerors. There are no statues of men you are meant to admire out of obligation. There is no guided tour unless you are the sort of person who goes about with a guided tour unspooling in your head. You do not buy tickets. You come on a Thursday afternoon when the laundromat is too bright and the clerk at the post office is on their smoke break. You don’t tell anyone you came.

Someone told you, years ago, that inventing a museum for your losses could help you survive them. That was back when you organized everything into drawers, lined socks in pairs like couples at a ballet, believed you could walk through a day by laying it out the night before. The museum outlasted those habits. Its rooms have kept multiplying, and you have learned to let them, the way a river learns to grow into its own curves.

In the foyer there is a bulletin board with a thumbtacked notice, typed on real paper, that says: If you are looking for a lost glove, it is never the first one you’ll find. People have added their own notes beneath it. A handwriting that looks like a careless sleepwalker’s says, When I broke the plate heirloom, it was the moment I felt free. Another, cramped and neat, says, Lost: the smell of old library paste. Will pay handsomely if found. There is a pen on a string, and a woman with a child writes slowly, her hand steady despite the boy digging his fingers into her sleeve. She writes, Lost: voice. Misplaced somewhere between evening news and bedtime.

You put your own hand on the pen and decide against it. You will not make a list. Lists have never saved you from the forgetting; they have only made it a documented failure.

The first room is all keys. They hang on nails, each with a yellow tag tied through the hole. Some are not keys to anything as it stands now: their teeth are wrong not because they were made wrong, but because their locks have aged into new shapes, nibbled at by weather and fingers and the seasons’ small, deliberate twisting. The tags carry notes, some in ballpoint, some typed, some with the thin, confident strokes of mechanical pencil. Back door to the bike shop, 1998. The gate we kept forgetting to lock until the night of the raccoons. The desk drawer where we kept the recipes on index cards that were actually just the same two recipes, rewritten whenever we found new cards we liked.

An old man with a knitted hat and a knotted scarf is staring at a key with a tag that says, Car, green hatchback, summer spent driving to the quarry, to the movies, to the blinking motel sign that said VACANCY like a benediction. He lifts the key off its nail and lets it lie across his palm. His mouth opens. The room absorbs his sigh like it is a teaspoon of sugar into warm tea.

You have no keys in this room, not the kind that turn in locks. But on your third visit you spot a shape that is both familiar and not—thin, stainless, a keycard to a building with a green-lit foyer and a mailroom door that always got stuck. Someone has written on the tag simply, Access. You have the impulse to slip it quietly into your pocket, to wander back to the old block and try it on the sensor by the call box, but the museum is gentle, and the impulse, not the theft, is what it preserves. You hang it neatly on its nail and move on.

There is a room of languages. It’s long as a corridor, smelling faintly of pencil shavings. Here is a shelf of dictionaries, papery as moths. Here is a line of mouth-shaped plaster castings, each with a bronze plaque below carrying a word you knew once and can now only summon the echo of. Words for weather with moods you’ve grown into and shed. Words for sorrow that sounded like a bell; for gladness that sounded like feet down a stair at suppertime. On a small pedestal, a glass dome protects a stack of flashcards whose edges have been nipped by living. A laminated card explains, without judgment, that all children are born with the capacity to make all the sounds of the world, and slowly, lovingly, narrow themselves to the household’s music. The narrowing is not loss, the card says. It is a choosing. But you and others have brought back words you dropped somewhere between a cafeteria and a youth you mistook for a lifetime.

Across the hall is the Room of Unsent Letters, and it breathes like paper. The air tastes like the glue of envelopes. There are thousands of pages arranged with surprising formality on long tables: the letter to a father you never wrote because he died while you were rehearsing it. The letter to the one you loved who did not love you, and the letter to the one you loved who did, and does, and to whom you cannot say the newest trembling of your heart because it seems childish and the children in your house demand all the loud, obvious love and leave no room for the childish. There are letters that begin, Dear Editor, and letters that begin, I would like to apologize for the time I, and letters that begin, I cannot do this anymore. Some are addressed to names you remember clearly. Some are addressed to a day or a window or a smell. A girl in a red hoodie picks one up that says, To the gap in the fence behind the church where we crawled through to skip choir. She bends to read and laughs. Her mother takes it from her hands and holds it so lightly, as if it might crack like an egg if pinched wrong, and says, quietly, Oh.

In the middle of the building is a courtyard with a single tree, the kind that blooms in spring with a flurry of small showy flowers and drops them at once as if it decided, mid-spectacle, that it had gone too far. The ground is paved with uneven bricks that tilt just enough that rainwater chooses its path in a new way every time, scribing its brief sentences. There is a bench that looks like the end of a pier, and often there is someone sitting on it with their eyes closed. The plaque says, The Place Where Things Return When We Stop Looking. No one has seen anything return here, at least not visibly. But there is a serenity to it, a waiting that is neither patient nor impatient, just untroubled by the idea of time scaling up or down from moment to moment.

Up the stairs and to the left is the Room of Lost Animals. You are not sure what you will find there because the name is so broad it could hold multitudes. Inside, it is quiet as snow. There are photographs on the walls of pets with eyes like polished buttons. There are bowls with names written in childish marker. A turtle shell rests under a card that says, He lived in our bathtub while we tried to figure out where he came from and if he had people. He ate strawberries like small red coins. One wall is a painted mural of a lake at dusk and a line of geese in arrow formation, and at the far end, in dim light, a case that is not glass, but something like it: a diorama you can enter if you are very certain you are ready. Inside, sounds are slowed, sorrow dilated like a pupil. There are creatures older than a map you know how to read, and empty spaces where something should be. They have not made a spectacle of extinction here; there are no species names in bold face, no didactics. Still, a weight drops into your tongue and tampers with your breath. You recognize an understanding you try to keep behind your eyes: loss is not always personal. Sometimes it is systemic enough to outlive your heart, and also your excuses.

You do not stay long. You walk into the Room of Lost Recipes and let the smell of cinnamon and overbrowned toast unspool your thinking. In here, four women argue mildly about whether nutmeg has any place in what they are making. A child is licking soup off her lower lip, grimacing. On a side table, a spiral notebook with a cover of sunflowers lies open. Its page shows a list in tight handwriting that says things like, add salt until it stops tasting like pepper, and if it looks sad, you probably forgot the lemon. A metal box of index cards is sectioned with worn cardboard tabs. Some of the recipes are full of measurements and insistence; others are a string of adjectives and one quiet command: stir more than you think. You realize that, like languages, recipes narrow with time, settle into a house’s preferred ways, until the person who knows them best leaves the house and takes the measure in their palm with them. A woman in a loose sweater stares into a pot of something boiling hard, then takes it off the heat and says to no one, I didn’t think about how no one would know how to make this once I am gone. She taps the edge of a spoon on the rim, the sound like a small knell, then turns to another woman and begins to narrate, slowly, in the way you speak to the present as if it were a child, what she is doing and why.

There is a Room of First Times. It’s darker than the others, lit with lamps that cast circles on the floor where you are meant to sit. There is a listening station, and in the headphones people whisper. The first time I rode a bicycle without stopping. The first time I understood that we all die. The first time a thing I wanted was given to me without my asking. The first time all the lights on the road caught green and I drove through them like a seal through sunlight. The first time I raised my voice and meant it like light coming through a cloud. Someone has written on the wall with a finger in dust, The first time I forgave what I thought was unforgivable. The dust holds the words as if it had been waiting for them for a long time. There are other firsts here that you walk by with your face averted, protections you have developed against the feeling of too many doors opening at once.

And of course there is a Room of Phones. People laugh when they step into it. There are rotary phones that murmur, landlines chopped off at the base like flowers, flip phones in satin jewel tones, smartphones with spider-webbed glass and phones so old they are more idea than instrument. On a shelf, a long row of chargers of every kind, their cords nested like eels. You watch a man in a suit press his face to a phone that is not his and close his eyes and say his own name into it. When he lifts his head there is a visible change in him, not relief, but something like the satisfaction of having rehearsed. You realize how thoroughly your life has been drawn through a single small object, all your roads and alleys of communication bundled like wires behind its screen. On a small pedestal is a phone that looks like an artifact from an early science fiction film, bulky, a lens on the front, an antenna standing like a question mark. The tag says, The one we used to call my grandmother in Thrissur. In my memory, every call was interrupted by cows. I used to think there were cows living in the wires.

You sit on a bench in the hallway between rooms and watch a docent who is older than the word docent polish a banister with a cloth that looks like it’s been used for a hundred years and is still exactly the right one for this work. She hums. You are seized, briefly, by the desire to be useful to the maintenance of what is revered. You offer to help, but she shakes her head without turning and says, almost kindly, Not today. Then she adds, Work is a sort of keeping, but so is being the person who leaves room.

There is a Room of Ideas You Believed Fervently and Then Let Go. This one makes you nervous. Inside, the shelves are labeled with the precision of a person who, once, believed in precise labelings. Belief that discipline alone saves. Belief that one person can be the reason you never suffer again. Belief that there is such a thing as someone who knows what to do about every circumstance. Belief that there is such a thing as having lived correctly. Belief that art must hurt to prove it’s serious. Belief that you can outrun the grief if you are faster than the days. People come in here and faint, sometimes, at the shock of recognition. There are chairs set around the room for this reason. On a wall is a long strip of paper, a timeline showing a gentle scatter of small black dots where beliefs faded, were replaced, evaporated, or were mislaid and never recovered. You write, with the supplied pencil, Belief that sincerity is ugly. Next to it, you add a check mark for the day you set that belief down, like a package you carried too long and realized did not belong to you.

In the basement is the Room of Tools. Here, the museum becomes more materially prickly. Hammers, planes, saws with teeth like tidy mountain ranges. Vises that flicker your memory of holding onto something too hard. Drawers of nails labeled by head and purpose. Chisels that have carved out the spaces where dovetails learn to kiss. A woman with her hair knotted in a bandana circles a lathe and touches it the way you touch the forehead of a sleeping child. On a long table in the middle, dozens of screwdrivers quiver with a desire to be chosen. It is a comfort to be in a room of things that exist explicitly to fix. You examine a box of spare parts—gaskets, cotter pins, washers like thin planets—and think about all the times you have cannibalized the functional for the sake of the immediate, the times you have raided the box and left it unbalanced. On the wall, a placard says, Maintenance is a form of love. The screws nod in their bin.

There is a Room of Names. It is full of them, printed and pressed in lead and carved with pocketknives into soft and hard surfaces alike. In the middle, a pillar is covered in chalk marks. People pick up chalk and write the names of those they cannot call out loud; this is both the point and the point’s undoing. There are pillows with names on them and paper slips folded like cranes, each wing tipped with a letter. A librarian in a wool skirt and sneakers walks slowly around and around the pillar like a planet, murmuring the names as if testing whether the syllables remember how to lean into each other. You look for your grandfather, your best friend from junior high, the dog whose name you said so often that you wore it smooth. You find them. Or you do not. In the absence, an ache opens that is both salt and tonic. It is the kindness of a room that expects you to feel what you feel without explaining it to anyone who is not yourself.

The Room of Lost Seasons is mostly a window. It frames an afternoon in autumn that has the color of old coins. Tomorrow it will be a summer storm so local you can see its edges and step in and out of it like a door to a cold room. Yesterday it was spring mud and the smell of worms. The plaque says nothing. A child in a green cap presses both hands to the glass and says, simply, Pickles. His mother looks at him, confused, and after a second says, Oh. Because she remembers a batch of garlicky refrigerator pickles and the day her son said the word first. The room gives back not the thing lost, but the sense that at some point it existed, and that alone is a kind of balm. The window is warm in summer and cold in winter, and the glass does not grow clouded when you breath against it; it seems to have learned long ago the trick of not fogging up with human words.

There is a Room of Numbers, too. It’s quieter than you would expect. Here are ledger books where someone once added columns and subtracted debts and steadied their hand in the steadiness of math. A chalkboard half-erased shows a formula that begins to solve for x and then writes the rest in a language that is not algebra. On a small table in the corner is an abacus that a father taught his son to use on Thursday nights between dinner and bedtime, then wrapped in a dishcloth and put up on the highest shelf. On a corkboard, cards with statistics, neatly printed: Number of days we didn’t speak. Number of times the phone rang and I didn’t answer because I thought it would be you. Number of times it was you and I did not answer anyway. Number of seeds in a tomato. Number of breaths taken between the nurse saying there is a problem and the surgeon saying we fixed it. Numbers that measure in other units than days: in laughter, in earthquakes, in jubilees, in fields of grain.

The museum has a Room of Second Chances, but it is the only room that is almost always closed. The sign on the handle says, Temporarily Unavailable, and no one who works here has ever been caught with a key. And yet people stand before it, hands on the wood, and it is evident that, in their own way, some version of that room opens for them, in their kitchens, in the crown of their skull, on the sidewalk when they look up and see a cloud that looks like a boat and remember that they once wanted to cross an ocean for no reason other than a desire to be on a boat crossing an ocean.

There is a Room of Paths Not Taken. Only a few brave teachers bring school groups through it. The floor is a map painted with lines in every color, some broken, some thick as arteries. The walls are lined with shoes, not worn through, but worn in the way a day gets worn when it has already been planned and executed. On a bench sits a violin that was played by a boy until his hands grew unaccountably clumsy in adolescence, and then placed under the bed in its case where it lay like sleeping thunder. A card says, The man who would have loved you but you didn’t trust, because you didn’t recognize love in that accent, with that haircut, at that time. Another says, The job you almost took in a small town where the river runs so clear you can count the pebbles even at dusk. It is not a room for regrets so much as one for honesty. If you stand still, you can feel the tick and hum of your own past decisions like a beehive in the wall, not threatening, exactly, but certainly alive.

At the farther end of the museum there is a room that is not listed on the map. You find it not by seeking but by walking behind a couple you don’t know very well and then losing track of them at a corner. The room is small. It has only a chair and a low table with a single object on it: a jar full of buttons. Not antique buttons, not particularly interesting. The kind that fall off shirts no one will make again. The kind you find in the bottom of your sewing box and pour into your palm and think, Now I am my grandmother. The label on the jar says, Time Set Aside For Repairs. You sit in the chair and realize that you have come to the exact border where one museum ends and another begins: the museum that houses what you will recover if you make the room for it. You pick up the jar and listen to the click of the buttons and think of all the things you do not mend because you have forgotten what it implies about your worth to repair instead of discard. You fill your pockets with a few of the buttons. There is no sign that forbids it. The museum does not punish the impulse to take what will be useful in another place.

On the way out you wander through the Room of Weather Reports. It is lined in pinboards where maps have been pricked in the way people prick the ears of dogs, lovingly, conspiratorially. There are printouts of the radar spirals of storms that altered the shape of entire coastlines, and on a table, a battered radio that, if you turn it to a certain notch, will play a recording of the long-ago voice that used to announce the barometric pressure in a city that fills your mouth with the taste of your old apartment’s dust. The museum does not make statements about climate. It lets the weather archive itself. And yet in the corner a crate holds postcards with the watercolor wash of wildfire skies and the back sides are peppered with messages from people who mailed them to themselves and then paid admission to come here and read them. You pick one up. It says, Look, we were not careful, but we were not malicious. Is there room in the ledger for that? You slide the card back under a handful of others and leave it there to be found by someone who thinks the same thing, or the opposite.

Near the exit there is a room that has no title. It contains a bed laid smooth with sheets, a stack of books on the floor, a sweater draped over a chair, and a bowl with three lemons. It looks like a life held in one glance. It looks like a set you could walk into and be inside of the sense you have some days that you almost remember something crucial that would make your days easier. A card on the wall has no explanation, only a question: Have you made enough space?

You say that you will come back. Everyone says that. And then you do, usually without meaning to. You find yourself in the Room of Glances, the one without signs, a corridor where people look at each other in a way that does not last long enough to be named, and you recognize the person whose mouth twitches like a sparrow’s when they lie, the one who has adopted the look of being unruffled and wears it like a collar. You see yourself reflected in a set of four mirrors that show you in the seasons of the day. You think, I am not who I was when I was in the Room of First Times. This feels like neither a betrayal nor a victory. It is an observation delivered with the tenderness of prophecy. You stand closer to the mirror until you can see the grains of your eyes and the small bright seed of your pupil. You look like someone who might someday decide to carry a tool roll in the trunk of their car. You look like someone who might plant a tree not for shade but for the feeling of having done it.

On your way down the stairs, you pass a mother teaching a child how to descend while holding the banister. The child’s hand slides and sticks and slides. This, too, will be stored somewhere. The museum is not greedy; it takes only what has enough echo to make a sound when it is set down. You hold the rail and let yourself be guided by the slope of the staircase. You step into the foyer where the bulletin board has acquired three new notes while you were upstairs. One says, Lost: the habit of checking whether the door is locked. Gained: the habit of turning the knob and trusting muscle memory. Another says, Lost: November. Found: a scarf at the bus stop that is not mine but looked like wanting to be.

There is a final room. If you ask the docent at the desk, she will only point vaguely, because it’s not the kind of room you find by being told. You find it because you have to put something down that is too heavy or too lovely or both. It has many doors because it is actually everywhere you choose to sit for a breath and close your eyes and practice not naming what is in your mouth like a river. Inside, the room contains a chair and a window and the view beyond that window is different every time. There is a small table. On it, a notebook and a pen. The notebook is for writing what you have identified not as lost, but as ongoing. It is for writing down the things you are not done with, and where to find them next. The idea that there is a place for this too—this living, this continuation—cracks open something in your sternum that you have kept shielded with a set of jokes and the knowledge of other people’s tragedies.

You do not take a photograph of the notebook. You do not take a photograph of yourself taking a photograph of the notebook. You sit down and write instead. You write, Tea with A. on Thursdays, and, The dusks in August that make everything golden in a way that is not false, and, The morning walks like counting backwards from ten. You leave the page open for the person who will come after you. You cap the pen and teeter for a second between the impulse to weep and the impulse to walk briskly out into the hall and make a list of chores. Both are decent impulses. The museum is generous with both.

When you finally leave, the threshold is the same one you crossed without noticing when you arrived. Outside, the city is itself—a little worn, a little ridiculous, a little relentless in its parade of need and invention. The sun is doing its habitual, unbelievable thing. Somewhere, a small dog is trying to bite a skateboard. Somewhere, a teenager is pressing their forehead against a cold window and vowing to never speak to their mother again and vowing, underneath, to love her forever. In the tree across the street, a bird is building a nest from the fraying end of someone’s blue yarn, and the nest will not be perfect, and it will be enough to keep the eggs from falling.

You adjust your coat. In your pocket, the buttons you took knock softly against each other like stones in shallow water. You are very slightly, unexpectedly relieved to carry their small weight. It is good, suddenly, to think of mending. It is good to imagine yourself, on some evening not yet scheduled, threading a needle and finding the buttonhole by feel.

At the corner, you wait for the light. A bus shudders by, full of bodies and bags and faces you could love if you had time to learn them. The wind lifts a curl of paper off the curb and flicks it against your shoe, and you stoop and pick it up. It is a receipt for oranges and milk and an item coded DMX-47 that could be a chicken or a bouquet or a mistake. You tuck it into your other pocket, not to archive, but to remind yourself that the world has always been a ledger of particulars, a ledger that you are writing and being written into without having to be omniscient or pure.

By the time you get to your door, you have half forgotten the feeling of the Room of Almost, its window and its snow that did not melt. But when you close your eyes to turn the key, something in you widens like a lens and takes in a little more light than it possibly needs. You stand for a breath in your own doorway and notice the exact sound the lock makes as it slips home. It is quieter than you expected. It is similar, but not identical, to the sound a button makes when it slips through its hole and holds. And it occurs to you that perhaps the museum is less a building than a discipline. That perhaps what you are learning is a way to pay attention to what leaves and a way to welcome what returns.

In the kitchen, the light throws itself across the counter with abandon. You set down your keys in their dish and take a jar from the cupboard, unscrew its lid, and pour in the three buttons. The sound they make on the glass is a small, precise clatter, like boot heels on a familiar stair. You label the jar in your head, though you do not write on it. You call it Time Set Aside For Repairs. You wash your hands. You stand with your palms braced on the counter and look out the window at the utility wires that trace long, dark cords across the sky. A sparrow lands, sways, rights itself. A cloud the shape of a roof drifts over. In the next room, something leans and corrects its balance, a picture frame maybe, or a stack of books quietly rehearsing the rules of gravity. You breathe. It is not that you have not lost things. It is that you are living. And the living, unruly keeper that it is, continues.

The docents will tell you this is an optical trick—the room has a door; your mind simply refuses to notice it, having been discouraged by years of glancing at exits and deciding they belong to other people in other emergencies. You put your own hand on the pen and decide against it. You will not make a list. Lists have never saved you from the forgetting; they have only made it a documented failure.

The first room is all keys. They hang on nails, each with a yellow tag tied through the hole. The tags carry notes, some in ballpoint, some typed, some with the thin, confident strokes of mechanical pencil. Back door to the bike shop, 1998. The desk drawer where we kept the recipes on index cards that were actually just the same two recipes, rewritten whenever we found new cards we liked.

An old man with a knitted hat and a knotted scarf is staring at a key with a tag that says, Car, green hatchback, summer spent driving to the quarry, to the movies, to the blinking motel sign that said VACANCY like a benediction. He lifts the key off its nail and lets it lie across his palm. His mouth opens. The room absorbs his sigh like it is a teaspoon of sugar into warm tea.

But on your third visit you spot a shape that is both familiar and not—thin, stainless, a keycard to a building with a green-lit foyer and a mailroom door that always got stuck. Someone has written on the tag simply, Access. You have the impulse to slip it quietly into your pocket, to wander back to the old block and try it on the sensor by the call box, but the museum is gentle, and the impulse, not the theft, is what it preserves. You hang it neatly on its nail and move on.

There is a room of languages. It’s long as a corridor, smelling faintly of pencil shavings. Here is a shelf of dictionaries, papery as moths. Here is a line of mouth-shaped plaster castings, each with a bronze plaque below carrying a word you knew once and can now only summon the echo of. Across the hall is the Room of Unsent Letters, and it breathes like paper. The air tastes like the glue of envelopes. There are thousands of pages arranged with surprising formality on long tables: the letter to a father you never wrote because he died while you were rehearsing it. The letter to the one you loved who did not love you, and the letter to the one you loved who did, and does, and to whom you cannot say the newest trembling of your heart because it seems childish and the children in your house demand all the loud, obvious love and leave no room for the childish. There are letters that begin, Dear Editor, and letters that begin, I would like to apologize for the time I, and letters that begin, I cannot do this anymore. Some are addressed to names you remember clearly. Some are addressed to a day or a window or a smell. A girl in a red hoodie picks one up that says, To the gap in the fence behind the church where we crawled through to skip choir. She bends to read and laughs. Her mother takes it from her hands and holds it so lightly, as if it might crack like an egg if pinched wrong, and says, quietly, Oh.

In the middle of the building is a courtyard with a single tree, the kind that blooms in spring with a flurry of small showy flowers and drops them at once as if it decided, mid-spectacle, that it had gone too far. The ground is paved with uneven bricks that tilt just enough that rainwater chooses its path in a new way every time, scribing its brief sentences. There is a bench that looks like the end of a pier, and often there is someone sitting on it with their eyes closed. The plaque says, The Place Where Things Return When We Stop Looking. No one has seen anything return here, at least not visibly. But there is a serenity to it, a waiting that is neither patient nor impatient, just untroubled by the idea of time scaling up or down from moment to moment.

Up the stairs and to the left is the Room of Lost Animals. You are not sure what you will find there because the name is so broad it could hold multitudes. Inside, it is quiet as snow. There are photographs on the walls of pets with eyes like polished buttons. There are bowls with names written in childish marker. A turtle shell rests under a card that says, He lived in our bathtub while we tried to figure out where he came from and if he had people. He ate strawberries like small red coins. One wall is a painted mural of a lake at dusk and a line of geese in arrow formation, and at the far end, in dim light, a case that is not glass, but something like it: a diorama you can enter if you are very certain you are ready. Inside, sounds are slowed, sorrow dilated like a pupil. There are creatures older than a map you know how to read, and empty spaces where something should be. They have not made a spectacle of extinction here; there are no species names in bold face, no didactics. Still, a weight drops into your tongue and tampers with your breath. You recognize an understanding you try to keep behind your eyes: loss is not always personal. Sometimes it is systemic enough to outlive your heart, and also your excuses.

You do not stay long. You walk into the Room of Lost Recipes and let the smell of cinnamon and overbrowned toast unspool your thinking. In here, four women argue mildly about whether nutmeg has any place in what they are making. A child is licking soup off her lower lip, grimacing. On a side table, a spiral notebook with a cover of sunflowers lies open. Its page shows a list in tight handwriting that says things like, add salt until it stops tasting like pepper, and if it looks sad, you probably forgot the lemon. A metal box of index cards is sectioned with worn cardboard tabs. Some of the recipes are full of measurements and insistence; others are a string of adjectives and one quiet command: stir more than you think. You realize that, like languages, recipes narrow with time, settle into a house’s preferred ways, until the person who knows them best leaves the house and takes the measure in their palm with them. A woman in a loose sweater stares into a pot of something boiling hard, then takes it off the heat and says to no one, I didn’t think about how no one would know how to make this once I am gone. She taps the edge of a spoon on the rim, the sound like a small knell, then turns to another woman and begins to narrate, slowly, in the way you speak to the present as if it were a child, what she is doing and why.

There is a Room of First Times. It’s darker than the others, lit with lamps that cast circles on the floor where you are meant to sit. There is a listening station, and in the headphones people whisper. The first time I rode a bicycle without stopping. The first time I understood that we all die. The first time a thing I wanted was given to me without my asking. The first time all the lights on the road caught green and I drove through them like a seal through sunlight. The first time I raised my voice and meant it like light coming through a cloud. Someone has written on the wall with a finger in dust, The first time I forgave what I thought was unforgivable. The dust holds the words as if it had been waiting for them for a long time. There are other firsts here that you walk by with your face averted, protections you have developed against the feeling of too many doors opening at once.

And of course there is a Room of Phones. People laugh when they step into it. There are rotary phones that murmur, landlines chopped off at the base like flowers, flip phones in satin jewel tones, smartphones with spider-webbed glass and phones so old they are more idea than instrument. On a shelf, a long row of chargers of every kind, their cords nested like eels. You watch a man in a suit press his face to a phone that is not his and close his eyes and say his own name into it. When he lifts his head there is a visible change in him, not relief, but something like the satisfaction of having rehearsed. You realize how thoroughly your life has been drawn through a single small object, all your roads and alleys of communication bundled like wires behind its screen. On a small pedestal is a phone that looks like an artifact from an early science fiction film, bulky, a lens on the front, an antenna standing like a question mark. The tag says, The one we used to call my grandmother in Thrissur. In my memory, every call was interrupted by cows. I used to think there were cows living in the wires.

You sit on a bench in the hallway between rooms and watch a docent who is older than the word docent polish a banister with a cloth that looks like it’s been used for a hundred years and is still exactly the right one for this work. She hums. You are seized, briefly, by the desire to be useful to the maintenance of what is revered. You offer to help, but she shakes her head without turning and says, almost kindly, Not today. Then she adds, Work is a sort of keeping, but so is being the person who leaves room.

There is a Room of Ideas You Believed Fervently and Then Let Go. This one makes you nervous. Inside, the shelves are labeled with the precision of a person who, once, believed in precise labelings. Belief that discipline alone saves. Belief that one person can be the reason you never suffer again. Belief that there is such a thing as someone who knows what to do about every circumstance. Belief that there is such a thing as having lived correctly. Belief that art must hurt to prove it’s serious. Belief that you can outrun the grief if you are faster than the days. People come in here and faint, sometimes, at the shock of recognition. There are chairs set around the room for this reason. On a wall is a long strip of paper, a timeline showing a gentle scatter of small black dots where beliefs faded, were replaced, evaporated, or were mislaid and never recovered. You write, with the supplied pencil, Belief that sincerity is ugly. Next to it, you add a check mark for the day you set that belief down, like a package you carried too long and realized did not belong to you.

In the basement is the Room of Tools. Here, the museum becomes more materially prickly. Hammers, planes, saws with teeth like tidy mountain ranges. Vises that flicker your memory of holding onto something too hard. Drawers of nails labeled by head and purpose. Chisels that have carved out the spaces where dovetails learn to kiss. A woman with her hair knotted in a bandana circles a lathe and touches it the way you touch the forehead of a sleeping child. On a long table in the middle, dozens of screwdrivers quiver with a desire to be chosen. It is a comfort to be in a room of things that exist explicitly to fix. You examine a box of spare parts—gaskets, cotter pins, washers like thin planets—and think about all the times you have cannibalized the functional for the sake of the immediate, the times you have raided the box and left it unbalanced. On the wall, a placard says, Maintenance is a form of love. The screws nod in their bin.

There is a Room of Names. It is full of them, printed and pressed in lead and carved with pocketknives into soft and hard surfaces alike. In the middle, a pillar is covered in chalk marks. People pick up chalk and write the names of those they cannot call out loud; this is both the point and the point’s undoing. There are pillows with names on them and paper slips folded like cranes, each wing tipped with a letter. A librarian in a wool skirt and sneakers walks slowly around and around the pillar like a planet, murmuring the names as if testing whether the syllables remember how to lean into each other. You look for your grandfather, your best friend from junior high, the dog whose name you said so often that you wore it smooth. You find them. Or you do not. In the absence, an ache opens that is both salt and tonic. It is the kindness of a room that expects you to feel what you feel without explaining it to anyone who is not yourself.

The Room of Lost Seasons is mostly a window. It frames an afternoon in autumn that has the color of old coins. Tomorrow it will be a summer storm so local you can see its edges and step in and out of it like a door to a cold room. Yesterday it was spring mud and the smell of worms. The plaque says nothing. A child in a green cap presses both hands to the glass and says, simply, Pickles. His mother looks at him, confused, and after a second says, Oh. Because she remembers a batch of garlicky refrigerator pickles and the day her son said the word first. The room gives back not the thing lost, but the sense that at some point it existed, and that alone is a kind of balm. The window is warm in summer and cold in winter, and the glass does not grow clouded when you breath against it; it seems to have learned long ago the trick of not fogging up with human words.

There is a Room of Numbers, too. It’s quieter than you would expect. Here are ledger books where someone once added columns and subtracted debts and steadied their hand in the steadiness of math. A chalkboard half-erased shows a formula that begins to solve for x and then writes the rest in a language that is not algebra. On a small table in the corner is an abacus that a father taught his son to use on Thursday nights between dinner and bedtime, then wrapped in a dishcloth and put up on the highest shelf. On a corkboard, cards with statistics, neatly printed: Number of days we didn’t speak. Number of times the phone rang and I didn’t answer because I thought it would be you. Number of times it was you and I did not answer anyway. Number of seeds in a tomato. Number of breaths taken between the nurse saying there is a problem and the surgeon saying we fixed it. Numbers that measure in other units than days: in laughter, in earthquakes, in jubilees, in fields of grain.

The museum has a Room of Second Chances, but it is the only room that is almost always closed. The sign on the handle says, Temporarily Unavailable, and no one who works here has ever been caught with a key. And yet people stand before it, hands on the wood, and it is evident that, in their own way, some version of that room opens for them, in their kitchens, in the crown of their skull, on the sidewalk when they look up and see a cloud that looks like a boat and remember that they once wanted to cross an ocean for no reason other than a desire to be on a boat crossing an ocean.

There is a Room of Paths Not Taken. Only a few brave teachers bring school groups through it. The floor is a map painted with lines in every color, some broken, some thick as arteries. The walls are lined with shoes, not worn through, but worn in the way a day gets worn when it has already been planned and executed. On a bench sits a violin that was played by a boy until his hands grew unaccountably clumsy in adolescence, and then placed under the bed in its case where it lay like sleeping thunder. A card says, The man who would have loved you but you didn’t trust, because you didn’t recognize love in that accent, with that haircut, at that time. Another says, The job you almost took in a small town where the river runs so clear you can count the pebbles even at dusk. It is not a room for regrets so much as one for honesty. If you stand still, you can feel the tick and hum of your own past decisions like a beehive in the wall, not threatening, exactly, but certainly alive.

At the farther end of the museum there is a room that is not listed on the map. You find it not by seeking but by walking behind a couple you don’t know very well and then losing track of them at a corner. The room is small. It has only a chair and a low table with a single object on it: a jar full of buttons. Not antique buttons, not particularly interesting. The kind that fall off shirts no one will make again. The kind you find in the bottom of your sewing box and pour into your palm and think, Now I am my grandmother. The label on the jar says, Time Set Aside For Repairs. You sit in the chair and realize that you have come to the exact border where one museum ends and another begins: the museum that houses what you will recover if you make the room for it. You pick up the jar and listen to the click of the buttons and think of all the things you do not mend because you have forgotten what it implies about your worth to repair instead of discard. You fill your pockets with a few of the buttons. There is no sign that forbids it. The museum does not punish the impulse to take what will be useful in another place.

On the way out you wander through the Room of Weather Reports. It is lined in pinboards where maps have been pricked in the way people prick the ears of dogs, lovingly, conspiratorially. There are printouts of the radar spirals of storms that altered the shape of entire coastlines, and on a table, a battered radio that, if you turn it to a certain notch, will play a recording of the long-ago voice that used to announce the barometric pressure in a city that fills your mouth with the taste of your old apartment’s dust. The museum does not make statements about climate. It lets the weather archive itself. And yet in the corner a crate holds postcards with the watercolor wash of wildfire skies and the back sides are peppered with messages from people who mailed them to themselves and then paid admission to come here and read them. You pick one up. It says, Look, we were not careful, but we were not malicious. Is there room in the ledger for that? You slide the card back under a handful of others and leave it there to be found by someone who thinks the same thing, or the opposite.

Near the exit there is a room that has no title. It contains a bed laid smooth with sheets, a stack of books on the floor, a sweater draped over a chair, and a bowl with three lemons. It looks like a life held in one glance. It looks like a set you could walk into and be inside of the sense you have some days that you almost remember something crucial that would make your days easier. A card on the wall has no explanation, only a question: Have you made enough space?

You say that you will come back. Everyone says that. And then you do, usually without meaning to. You find yourself in the Room of Glances, the one without signs, a corridor where people look at each other in a way that does not last long enough to be named, and you recognize the person whose mouth twitches like a sparrow’s when they lie, the one who has adopted the look of being unruffled and wears it like a collar. You see yourself reflected in a set of four mirrors that show you in the seasons of the day. You think, I am not who I was when I was in the Room of First Times. This feels like neither a betrayal nor a victory. It is an observation delivered with the tenderness of prophecy. You stand closer to the mirror until you can see the grains of your eyes and the small bright seed of your pupil. You look like someone who might someday decide to carry a tool roll in the trunk of their car. You look like someone who might plant a tree not for shade but for the feeling of having done it.

On your way down the stairs, you pass a mother teaching a child how to descend while holding the banister. The child’s hand slides and sticks and slides. This, too, will be stored somewhere. The museum is not greedy; it takes only what has enough echo to make a sound when it is set down. You hold the rail and let yourself be guided by the slope of the staircase. You step into the foyer where the bulletin board has acquired three new notes while you were upstairs. One says, Lost: the habit of checking whether the door is locked. Gained: the habit of turning the knob and trusting muscle memory. Another says, Lost: November. Found: a scarf at the bus stop that is not mine but looked like wanting to be.

There is a final room. If you ask the docent at the desk, she will only point vaguely, because it’s not the kind of room you find by being told. You find it because you have to put something down that is too heavy or too lovely or both. It has many doors because it is actually everywhere you choose to sit for a breath and close your eyes and practice not naming what is in your mouth like a river. Inside, the room contains a chair and a window and the view beyond that window is different every time. There is a small table. On it, a notebook and a pen. The notebook is for writing what you have identified not as lost, but as ongoing. It is for writing down the things you are not done with, and where to find them next. The idea that there is a place for this too—this living, this continuation—cracks open something in your sternum that you have kept shielded with a set of jokes and the knowledge of other people’s tragedies.

You do not take a photograph of the notebook. You do not take a photograph of yourself taking a photograph of the notebook. You sit down and write instead. You write, Tea with A. on Thursdays, and, The dusks in August that make everything golden in a way that is not false, and, The morning walks like counting backwards from ten. You leave the page open for the person who will come after you. You cap the pen and teeter for a second between the impulse to weep and the impulse to walk briskly out into the hall and make a list of chores. Both are decent impulses. The museum is generous with both.

When you finally leave, the threshold is the same one you crossed without noticing when you arrived. Outside, the city is itself—a little worn, a little ridiculous, a little relentless in its parade of need and invention. The sun is doing its habitual, unbelievable thing. Somewhere, a small dog is trying to bite a skateboard. Somewhere, a teenager is pressing their forehead against a cold window and vowing to never speak to their mother again and vowing, underneath, to love her forever. In the tree across the street, a bird is building a nest from the fraying end of someone’s blue yarn, and the nest will not be perfect, and it will be enough to keep the eggs from falling.

You adjust your coat. In your pocket, the buttons you took knock softly against each other like stones in shallow water. You are very slightly, unexpectedly relieved to carry their small weight. It is good, suddenly, to think of mending. It is good to imagine yourself, on some evening not yet scheduled, threading a needle and finding the buttonhole by feel.

At the corner, you wait for the light. A bus shudders by, full of bodies and bags and faces you could love if you had time to learn them. The wind lifts a curl of paper off the curb and flicks it against your shoe, and you stoop and pick it up. It is a receipt for oranges and milk and an item coded DMX-47 that could be a chicken or a bouquet or a mistake. You tuck it into your other pocket, not to archive, but to remind yourself that the world has always been a ledger of particulars, a ledger that you are writing and being written into without having to be omniscient or pure.

By the time you get to your door, you have half forgotten the feeling of the Room of Almost, its window and its snow that did not melt. But when you close your eyes to turn the key, something in you widens like a lens and takes in a little more light than it possibly needs. You stand for a breath in your own doorway and notice the exact sound the lock makes as it slips home. It is quieter than you expected. It is similar, but not identical, to the sound a button makes when it slips through its hole and holds. And it occurs to you that perhaps the museum is less a building than a discipline. That perhaps what you are learning is a way to pay attention to what leaves and a way to welcome what returns.

In the kitchen, the light throws itself across the counter with abandon. You set down your keys in their dish and take a jar from the cupboard, unscrew its lid, and pour in the three buttons. The sound they make on the glass is a small, precise clatter, like boot heels on a familiar stair. You label the jar in your head, though you do not write on it. You call it Time Set Aside For Repairs. You wash your hands. You stand with your palms braced on the counter and look out the window at the utility wires that trace long, dark cords across the sky. A sparrow lands, sways, rights itself. A cloud the shape of a roof drifts over. In the next room, something leans and corrects its balance, a picture frame maybe, or a stack of books quietly rehearsing the rules of gravity. You breathe. It is not that you have not lost things. It is that you are living. And the living, unruly keeper that it is, continues.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
